text: The surgeon incises the gastrohepatic ligament to expose the curl fibers running caudally over the aorta. The gastrohepatic ligament is peeled off the curl fibers, with the dissection progressing cephalad.

The surgeon continues the dissection cephalad, exposing the posterior wall of the stomach on the lesser curve. The curl fibers are retracted to the left, and the dissection progresses through the retrogastric tunnel towards the greater curve, aiming to encircle the gastroesophageal junction with a Penrose drain.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect along the greater curvature, dividing the short gastric vessels and progressing towards the angle of His and the esophageal hiatus. The dissection is performed high above the spleen, meeting the previous dissection under the gastrohepatic ligament. A grasper is passed from the lesser to the greater curve to encircle the gastrohepatic ligament and gastroesophageal junction with a Penrose drain, retracting it towards the patient's left shoulder.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum over the gastrohepatic ligament to increase laxity, then proceeds to take down additional retrogastric attachments. This allows the stomach, esophagus, and gastrohepatic ligament to be retracted away from the crural fibers. The focus then shifts to dissecting the crura off the aorta.

The surgeon attempts to develop a plane between the right and left crura, aiming to expose the aortic adventitia. The crura are visible as distinct anatomical structures at the beginning of the dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects layer by layer, pushing the right cruce to the left and the left cruce to the right, revealing the pearly white adventitia overlying the pulsating aorta. The dissection continues caudad, dividing fibers of the left cruce as they cross over the aorta, until the origin of the celiac axis is visible.

The surgeon divides the remaining fibers of the left cruce and the median arcuate ligament, relieving compression on the origin of the celiac axis. The celiac artery is being skeletonized, with loose areolar tissue being carefully removed from both sides.

The diaphragmatic crura are fully freed, ensuring no compression on the celiac axis. The median arcuate ligament tissue is completely divided, allowing unobstructed flow through the celiac artery. The surgeon continues to skeletonize the artery, leaving it free of any overlying tissue as it exits the aorta.
The surgeon re-approximates the divided crura above the celiac artery using interrupted figure-of-eight sutures with O-ethabond to prevent a hiatal hernia.
The surgeon places a few interrupted sutures to close the surgical site. After ensuring the closure is satisfactory, the retractor and ports are removed, marking the conclusion of the operation.
